A comparison of the number of cycles to rupture at low strains for cycle times of 2 and 30 min revealed that long cycle times decrease the strain absorption capacity.

A severe frequency effect, for example, was observed at $1\%$ strain and at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ; fine-grained material endured approximately 700 cycles at 2 min per cycle and only 160 cycles at 30 min per cycle.
At $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , however, 2- and 30-min cycles yielded the same number of cycles to rupture at $1\%$ strain.

Data obtained at $1600^{\circ}F$ on specimens stress cycled at 1 and 10 cps revealed a similar frequency effect to that observed in the strain cycling tests conducted at ORNL.
Specimens cycled at the high frequency endured 10 times more cycles before failure than did the specimens in comparable lower frequency tests.
Conventional Goodman-type diagrams, which relate time to rupture to the stress ratio, were obtained for the two frequencies, 1 and 10 cps.
Multiaxially stressed tubular specimens were creep tested at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
It was found that the creep rate in the axial direction was not influenced by tangential stress in cases where the axial-to-tangential stress ratio, $\sigma_z / \sigma_{t'}$ , was greater than 0.5.
At the stress ratio of 0.5, no axial creep was observed, although a tensile stress was present in the axial direction.
At stress ratios, $\sigma_z / \sigma_{t'}$ from 0.5 to -1.0, compressive axial creep was observed.
As the stress ratio decreased from $\infty$ to 1.0, for example, when one of the principal stresses was held at 6000 psi, the time to rupture decreased from 350 to 90 hr.

At ratios below 1.0, where only the axial stress was varied, the variance of time to rupture with stress ratio was only slight, although the time to rupture continued to decrease as the stress ratio decreased.
The minimum time to rupture occurred at the stress ratio -1.0.
It seems likely that deformation rates under multiaxial stresses are controlled by the maximum normal stress in the axial direction in the range of stress ratios from 0.5 to $\infty$ .

text: Since conversion of $\mathrm{Y}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ and suitable compounds of magnesium to fluorides can be accomplished on a large scale in available equipment, a pilot plant capable of supplying a quantity of fluoride mixture sufficient for the production of 200 lb of yttrium metal per month has been designed and is being constructed.
Therefore conditions for the treatment of $\mathsf{Y}_2\mathsf{O}_3$ by HF have been examined in small-scale equipment similar to the 250-lb-scale equipment which was
The treatment temperatures used have varied from 1100 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and HF flow rates have varied between 84 and 132 liters/hr for a 3-lb charge of $\mathsf{Y}_2\mathsf{O}_3$ .
The processing time was usually 12 hr, but one test was terminated after 6 hr.

Some further testing will be required to establish optimum processing times and HF flow rates, but the results show that a temperature of $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and the lowest flow rate of HF used thus far are adequate.
The scale-up of the process to that to be used in the very similar 250-lb-capacity equipment should present no great difficulty.
A temperature of $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was found to be adequate for conversion of either magnesium compound.
The conversion, based on the fluorine-to-magnesium ratio obtained from chemical analyses, was above $95\%$ in every case and was, in general, better than $98\%$ .
It appears that sufficiently good conversion to provide feed for the liquid-phase purification step can be assured and that scale-up to 250-lb equipment will not prove to be difficult.

One pump has operated under cavitation conditions for more than 4000 hr without changes in performance characteristics and the other has operated 500 hr.

Valves with the plug and the seat of Stellar 6 overlaid on Inconel have been tested with NaK, and valves with Kennametal cermets for both the plug and the seat have been tested with fused salts.
The valve with the Stellar 6 plug and seat was tested with NaK at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ under a pressure of 50 psi.
Leakage decreased from $0.75~\mathrm{cm}^3 /\mathrm{hr}$ to $0.2~\mathrm{cm}^3 /\mathrm{hr}$ after 1500 hr.
The force needed to open the valve remained less than 50 lb, and a 1300-lb force was needed to effect closure.
The valve with the K-162B cermet seat (Rockwell A hardness, 89) and KM plug (Rockwell A hardness, 91) operated satisfactorily when the test liquid was free of particulate matter.
